items.correct: A cadence ending on chord V, creating a sense of incompleteness.
items.misconception: A cadence that sounds wrong or is performed badly.
items.random_wrong: A cadence using only major chords.
items.why: Students think 'imperfect' means flawed. It's a technical term for an unfinished-sounding cadence - any chord moving to V leaves the music suspended, awaiting resolution.

items.correct: Two or more independent melodic lines sounding together.
items.misconception: A single melody with chord accompaniment.
items.why: Students confuse polyphony with thick texture or homophony. Polyphony specifically means INDEPENDENT MELODIES - like a fugue. Melody + chords is HOMOPHONY, not polyphony.

items.q: How does D Dorian differ from D minor?
items.correct: Dorian has a raised 6th (B natural) compared to natural minor.
items.random_wrong: Dorian has no sharps or flats at all.
items.why: Students treat modes as variants of major/minor. Dorian on D uses only white keys (like C major, but starting on D), giving a raised 6th compared to D natural minor - producing the modal flavour.

items.q: Whose 1725 Gradus ad Parnassum codified species counterpoint?
items.why: It's Fux. “Rameau” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which famous four-note motif begins Beethoven's Fifth?
items.why: Correct answer: Short-short-short-long. “Long-short-short” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Bach compose the Goldberg Variations?
items.why: The real figure is 1741 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
